# COLOR WHEEL WEDNESDAY ep.1

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube Shorts)
**Date:** 2026-03-11
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/shorts/_3-s2gM9MIM

## Description

Ostwald's color system, with its unique 24-hue color wheel, was very popular in the 1930s and 1940s. So what happened?

## Transcript

Hey, it's color wheel Wednesday. It's the thing I'm doing now. If I remember to. We'll see. We'll see how it goes. If you like it, I'll keep doing it.

This week's color wheel is the Oswald hue circle. So, we're going to go back to the source — _The Color Primer_. This is a 14th edition from 1930, which as far as I know was the last edition that Wilhelm Oswald personally oversaw the production of before his death in 1932.

So, let's go ahead and open this up to page 19 where we find the 24-hue Oswald circle. It's not a red, yellow, blue wheel. It's not a pigment mixing wheel. It is a psychological wheel or a perceptual wheel. So, he's got this lemon yellow across from ultramarine blue, not across from a violet. He actually called this "oo," not ultramarine. And he called this "crest," not orange, because like ultramarine and orange, these words were too French — and he developed his system during World War I when French and German relations were, shall we say, not very good.

This guy Taylor translated Wilhelm Oswald's works into English and sort of promoted this. This is actually published by Windsor and Newton. You get all of these Oswald-based materials, not only being sponsored by paint companies like Windsor and Newton, but also Reeves, which sponsored this one by Arthur McMorland. And you can see he's using the 24-hue circle and the 8-hue Oswald circle.

The English schools also saw a lot of adoption of the Oswald system in the 1930s. And this is Judson's _Handbook of Color_, again printed in England. And again, the Oswald circle with 24 standard hues.

In the 1930s and 40s, the key promoter of Oswald's system in the United States was Egert Jacobson. Here's the 24 standard hues in his book. Even as late as the 1960s, American companies were using Oswald's system to sort of standardize their color offerings. This is Higgins Inks from 1964. And you can actually still get leaf green in Higgins inks — it's a very Oswald color.

But what happened? Why is this not used anymore? Other systems took the place of Oswald. And the main reason that it's sort of unwieldy and not easy to update and adapt is that it's a closed system of standards. So if you come up with a new more vibrant ink, there's like nowhere to put it in the system.

| ------------------------------------- | --------------- | ---- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| _Farbenlehre_ (5 vols.)               | Wilhelm Ostwald | 1918 | [archive.org](https://archive.org/details/farbenlehrevonwi12ostw)    | [pdfs/ostwald-farbenlehre-1918.pdf](pdfs/ostwald-farbenlehre-1918.pdf)                         |
| _Einführung in die Farbenlehre_       | Wilhelm Ostwald | 1919 | [archive.org](https://archive.org/details/einfhrungindief00ostwgoog) | [pdfs/ostwald-einfuehrung-farbenlehre-1919.pdf](pdfs/ostwald-einfuehrung-farbenlehre-1919.pdf) |
| _The Color Primer_ (Faber Birren ed.) | Wilhelm Ostwald | 1969 | [archive.org](https://archive.org/details/colorprimerbasic0000ostw)  | borrow-only                                                                                    |
| _The Color Primer_ (alt. edition)     | Wilhelm Ostwald | 1969 | [archive.org](https://archive.org/details/ostwaldcolorprim0000unse)  | borrow-only                                                                                    |
| _Letters to a Painter_                | Wilhelm Ostwald | 1907 | [archive.org](https://archive.org/details/letterstoapaint00ostwgoog) | [pdfs/ostwald-letters-to-a-painter-1907.pdf](pdfs/ostwald-letters-to-a-painter-1907.pdf)       |
